Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course's focus on 'global asset management' and 'financial management' directly supports economic growth (Target 8.1) and strengthening financial institutions (Target 8.10). Model A revised to align with Model B's stronger reasoning.

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: 'Global asset allocation' is fundamental to financing infrastructure (Target 9.1) and industrial enterprises (Target 9.3). 'Sustainability considerations' (B/G) supports sustainable industrialization (Target 9.4).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course's focus on 'global asset allocation' and 'asset pricing' is central to understanding and improving the regulation of global financial markets (Target 10.5), which can help reduce inequalities. Model A revised to align with Model B's stronger reasoning.

Model A revised to align with Model B's strong reasoning.

Climate Action.reason: The core competency on 'sustainability considerations' (B/G) can be inferred to include climate risk in financial management, indirectly supporting climate action (Target 13.2). Model A revised to align with Model B's reasoning.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The core competency on 'ethics' (B/G) directly addresses reducing corruption (Target 16.5). The study of global finance is relevant to combating illicit financial flows (Target 16.4). Model A revised to align with Model B's strong reasoning.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course's focus on 'global asset management,' 'global asset allocation,' and 'foreign exchange risk' directly relates to mobilizing financial resources (Target 17.3) and enhancing global macroeconomic stability (Target 17.13). Model A revised to align with Model B's strong reasoning.
